speaker: interviewer
text: You were asked to testify against Moll--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the trial.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell-- tell me what happened then.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to the platform. The same routine was, I had to point with my finger and show him. And then the prosecutor started asking me questions about Moll. What type of person he was, what type of man.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had to tell them the truth, you know, that Moll, he used to kill the prisoners on the working place where we were working. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He couldn't stay a minute still without trying to kill a prisoner. He took prisoners. He beat them to death, and he pushed them in the cement, because they were building that big factory and it was so much cement. The tops that they were-- they didn't want any, you know, no bomb to go through. So he used to throw in prisoners in the cement and bury them [? there, ?] and just write in the report, prisoner drowned in cement. If they are gonna-- maybe they did break all the cement work, but we are working there weeks and months. Maybe they took out the prisoners from there. If not, the prisoners are laying dead in the cement with no mercy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they used to giggle and laugh and call his comerades, the other Germans, to show them what he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the defense attorneys ask you about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't ask me absolutely nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did other witnesses testify against Moll also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a farmer came. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A civilian, a farmer. And the farmer had a farm near where I was liberated, near-- close to Bad Tolz. And he came. And he was testifying against Moll. And he says, he cannot sleep nights, the farmer said, knowing it, that he hears maybe 30 or 40 prisoners shot on his property in his yard in his-- on his lawn.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, how do you know who killed them? He says, I know. He spoke to me quite a while. I gave him water, and I gave him something to eat. He came into my house. And I know [? well he ?] did it. He lined up the people, and they shot them cold dead, and they buried them. Other prisoners had to bury all the people he shot. So the prosecutor-- the defense man said, who is this? You go and show. You have to point them out. He came. And he showed, you know, this is the man, Moll. So comparing-- that's what I tell you-- comparing my details, which is terrible, murder, horrible, the worst stories that you can pull out your hair from your head. The farmer, a German himself, says he was trembling. He's still trembling. He wants the bodies to be removed from the property. That was his claim. And that man shot them single-handed. How many people testified against each criminal?

speaker: interviewee
text: All these-- all the 40 people? I cannot tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how many people do you think testified against Moll?

speaker: interviewee
text: Against Moll? Maybe eight or 10 people, at least.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear each person?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it took-- it took almost all day for one man sometime to testify. Moll took hours and hours, because he had such a history of being a murderer and being, you know, SS, with a long, long history, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- were-- how many hours were you on the stand testifying against Moll?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been, like, a half hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Johann Kirsch, that was a lot longer. Maybe two hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did many people testify against Johann Kirsch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, all the doctors and the lawyers and everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hours?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was each prisoner, like, tried on a different day? How did that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's not-- randomly, you know. When it came to one prisoner, they started with the prisoner, and they ended with the prisoner. Then they go another two, three, four, like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did each prisoner take more than a day or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It took-- yeah, that's why it took almost six weeks. It took a long time. I don't remember exactly. But it took so much time till the trial was finished, because prisoners were bought from other countries. They were already settled. They were in civilian clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the trial began, all 40 prisoners were there, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: All there. Day by day, they were all there, sitting in the same place with the same numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did each prisoner have a different attorney?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't have that. It was American defenders.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many defenders were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see no civilian-- I don't remember seeing any defenders' private lawyers there. I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many different prosecutors were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: American prosecutors. Yeah, it was plenty. Maybe five or six. It was a whole table long with all American high rank law people, you know. It started with the prosecutor, which he was, I think, a general. I don't know what he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a jury?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who tried the case? Who was in charge of--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Americans tried the case. Only the Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, was there a judge or more than one judge? How-- how did that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who made the decision?

speaker: interviewee
text: --yes, it was-- I think a judge was there. A judge was there. He gave the knock with the gavel at the end of the trial and they were all sentenced.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to that, how many people did you testify against?

speaker: interviewee
text: I testified against Johann Kirsch, Moll, Temple, and Kramer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Temple, what was the-- what did you say about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Temple, that was a-- the-- a wild mad dog. He didn't know what to do, only kill people all day long in the yard, in the-- in-- in-- in-- in the camps. He-- he was beating people, with no mercy, to death, day and night. He could walk in at night, and people was sleeping, and drag you out in the street in the snow from nowheres, and kill and knock you with anything in the head and kill you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the defense attorney question your testimony about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was more than me to testify about Temple, you understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Kramer?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Temple, personally, I didn't have as much as I had on the Commandant Johann Kirsch. I was lucky not to fall into his hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Kramer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kramer, not too much. Too-- terrible murderer. Only killing and making speeches make us like you're nobodies, like you ain't got got no chance to come out alive. And to [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] tell his stories, how many killed and how many he's gonna kill. It was like pleasure for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- before the sentencing--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did any of the defendants have a chance to speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they present their case?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they had. They had.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that work? How-- how did--

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing almost like Nuremberg, one blamed on the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they actually-- each individual took the stand--

speaker: interviewee
text: The stand.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or was able to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, some say-- say-- didn't say a word. [SPEAKING GERMAN] Got orders. [SPEAKING GERMAN] Got orders. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. They all try to-- like, they all get to do their discipline of work. That was the system.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Where you there for the sentencing? Were you in the room for the sentencing?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the sentencing? No, I was not in the room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what happened. I think I didn't feel good or something like that. I wasn't the last days in the-- in the-- in the sentencing room. But my friend, the doctors, told me what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still at the base?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, I was right there. I didn't see him hanging either. Nobody saw him hanging. That's why a lot of my friends who were with me in the camps together in Dachau think, you know, that it was not [INAUDIBLE], because it was never shown on TV, nothing. It was never even made a big fuss about it. All the Nuremberg Trials, of course, was the biggest thing, but at least prisoners who were suffering and all that thing, they-- they should have aired some show sometimes on TV or see something. Didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, perhaps one day, it will be.

speaker: interviewee
text: And see. And there is all in the archives, there is laying all-- maybe getting dust on it. And nobody's ever asking or demanding it should be shown, you know, till all the people die out, you know, the ex-prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the trial was over, what-- what did you do next?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it comes to me now to my mind. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah. Because my-- I was at the trial, then it was sentence. I'm [? sad. ?] And you know, it's so many years, it keeps out. It keeps out, yeah. Because I heard the words, to be hanged to death until death. Yeah, I was [? at it ?] till the end. yeah, I was. But I was already so much confused maybe that I was like in a-- like flying. But I heard the words. I was in court, yeah, till the last day, till the sentence they [? says, ?] because I heard-- it's ringing right in my ear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell me. Tell me everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was ringing. Everybody when they stood up, Johann Kirsch sentence to be dying, to be hang until death. So God help you. Only four came. And that-- now it's coming to me. Four was-- one was sentenced, like, 20 years, and another one 20 years, and another, like, five years. These were, like, little guys, you know, in the camp there. Had to do with the guards to change or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did it work? Did they call each person's name and-- and

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, called each person's name, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- what-- how did they react? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They just stare down their head. That's why I say, Kramer made a speech when he was called up. And they made a speech when they told him. You see, sometimes your-- your brain locks on you. When you have so many things, it locks on you. I heard Kramer making the speech. He said, I hated Jews. I never liked Jews. I killed Jews. And I'm going to die hating them. Heil Hitler. And he [INAUDIBLE]. He's the only one who made a speech sentencing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody else say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction to the people in the-- of the people in the courtroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was silent. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was very silent. I think it was told not to make no big commotions or something like that. It was ordered. It was silent. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was silent. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Only the noise what we heard was made from the people who write, you know, in the newspapers. [? This were ?] all among them making noise. And the people, you know, the American [INAUDIBLE], they just-- after the sentence, they all got up and walked out. And that was the end. And then all these prisoners were taken away. We didn't see it. We were all out of the court. But seeing directly hanging there, nobody saw it. That's why a lot of people think the way it looks, maybe they were not even hanged. But it doesn't work in American courts like that. If it's said hanged, he's hanged.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Kirsch's family in the courtroom that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see them. I didn't see them. I saw them one day. They were sitting close to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: But later on in the court, they must have sit in the other places. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- what became of your life after the trial? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to Sankt Ottilien. And I stayed there maybe another five, six months to recuperate. And then we were settled to that villa [INAUDIBLE], what I told you at the beginning. And we were staying there and learn agriculture. And then we made out papers. I sent, to my uncle, letters if he can bring me to America. And then came the Hagganah. Ben-Gurion came to Sankt Ottilien, and he made a speech. I stood right near him, right near Ben-Gurion. He made a speech. Because Israel was not a country that time yet. And they made speeches.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I sent letters to my uncle. And my uncle start to make papers. And I [? ask. ?] He was a very poor man, so it was harder, you know? He had a small shop. Because you had to have a guarantee that you bring to the United States that you can supply them with work. And my uncle was like almost like a one-man shop. He had the same trade like me, woodturning. And the pool cues. He used to make the billiards sticks. So it took a long time till I got my papers to come to the United States. And my uncle was told that I'm going to leave Germany. And before I left Germany, a CIA man came to my home. I was living there no more at the villa [INAUDIBLE]. I was living in a private room.

speaker: interviewee
text: The villa [INAUDIBLE] was given away then already to the family. Little by little, you know, the Germans start to get back, you understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a CIA man came with a dog and a Jeep. And I lived in a German house. If the German would said one word about me that I'm not a good man, they wouldn't let me into the United States. And I felt bad about that. That part, I felt bad, that CIA was questioning a German. He was not the SS man, but he was still a German and he was a [? sympatico ?] and for Hitler in the good days. Of what did you hear about Moses? Does he behave good? Is he a honest man? So luckily, the people, these Germans liked me, so they tell a good opinion on me. So the German [INAUDIBLE] came with a typewriter right in the room and he wrote everything. And they had a German dog with them in the Jeep. And they wrote up. He says, and he gave me a knock on the shoulder, I think we'll see in America. And I got the papers, then months and months. Took maybe a-- oh, maybe nine or 10 months, maybe a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the 24th of July, 1949, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I came to the United States. And my uncle drove from the Bronx, he lived, all the way to Boston to pick me up in Boston. And then I saw him through the fences. A lot of people from the [INAUDIBLE] was there giving out donuts to the people, and milk, who just arrived. And through the fence, I saw the profile. I said, oh, that is my uncle. He looks a little like-- he looks like my father. And a lot of people who came from Germany, you know, they were grown up, all the people, says [INAUDIBLE] they had with them big, you know, boxes with clothes, with with bicycles, with all kind of things. I came back-- [? back ?] with a little satchel, you know, like you go-- like a lawyer walks with a little thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said to me, Morris, where is your boxes? He saw people dragging boxes. I says, I don't have no boxes. That's what I have is from a care package, shaving cream, toothbrush, toothpaste. He says, that's what you own? He says, OK, as long as you are here. He was a poor man. So he was driving all the way from Boston-- from the Bronx to Boston to pick me up in a very old car. And going back to Boston, the car was heating up. He had to pour oil every hour. And every hour, as was pouring oil in the car, it was very hot that year in 1949. A historic hot day was in 1949 in July. So he was drinking cola. And he says, Morris, [? Moshe, ?] drink a cola too. Tasted very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: But after I came in the Bronx, for two weeks, I couldn't sleep from the Coca-Cola. It kept me awake. I didn't know that they had caffeine. And I started work for my uncle. He had a shop on the Bowery. And he cried very much that he lost my father, his brother, and he lost his father, my grandfather, and he lost his sister, my aunt, in Lithuania. And he says, at least, he says, some of my family survived. I'll try to help you what I can. I'm not a rich man, you see. And he gave me a job in his shop. And I was working. I was making $30 a week. And he says, $8. I rented a room on Morris Avenue in the Bronx. You'll-- you'll pay the landlady $8 a week. You'll give my wife-- that was his second wife-- you'll give her $8 a week for making your breakfast. And when you come from work, you'll have dinner with us. So it's $16. And you [? live-- ?] every day, it'll cost you $0.50 on the Bowery. You'll buy corned beef and a drink. It'll cost you $0.50. So you'll still save up, he says, maybe $8, $7 for the bank every week. And you can go in in a movie, and you can go sometime and take a haircut. And he took out $5 from his pocket. And he says, Morris, a man in the United States doesn't walk around without money. I came to the United States, I didn't have $0.05.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wrote a letter and I wanted to send it over. I didn't have money for a stamp. And my cousin asked me, what are you walking around with that letter? I didn't know a stamp [INAUDIBLE]. I says, I need a market. He says, a stamp? Let's go to the post office. And I wrote a letter to Italy, to my sister, that I arrived in the United States safely. And he says, Morris, men in the America doesn't walk around without money. And he opened up my hand and he stuck $5 in my hand, and he closed my hand like that, strong, like the old-timers, you know? And he says, take a week and go on my daughter's farm in Freehold in New Jersey. Take a rest. And after the rest in New Jersey, she has a chicken farm, you'll come, and we're gonna start working.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked for my uncle a year. And I know he tried to help me, but he was not too rich. So I said to him, uncle, you wouldn't mind if I-- people offer me bigger jobs with more intricate work than pool cues, you know, real architectural, industrial turnings. And I can do that if I go to work somewhere else. I don't want to leave you, uncle. He had a black man helping him all the time for many years. So the black man said to me, Moses, go, go. You'll make three times the money. I see what kind of mechanic you are. Maybe five times. Go. It's America. Everybody needs money.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went out to lunch to eat. And I went over to another place on Lafayette. Houston Street near Lafayette. I walked over there to a shop, and I walked in. I said, I'm a woodturner. So the boss looked and he-- and he started a little laughing, because he knows woodturners are old men, 50, 60, 40, 55. Here comes a [INAUDIBLE], a boy. He see with long tools like [INAUDIBLE]. I made my own tools, long ones. He says, you're a woodturner? He says, where did you work? I says, in the billiard-- New York Billiard and Table Company on the Bowery. He says, oh, for [? Isa ?] That's my uncle's name. Yes. I says, why don't you work anymore there? I said, it's kind of slow.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, go to the lathe. Ask [? Mark. ?] There's the foreman, [? Mark, ?] he'll give you the lathe. I went to the lathe. And he gave me a blueprint. And [INAUDIBLE] I took out my tools. The other woodturners, who stayed nearby by the other lathes, start to giggle and laugh and making jokes out of me, because I took out such a big long tools, homemade.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they thought I'm German. And they said, what is that German going to do with this [? pieces ?] [? iron ?] here? But when I had a piece of wood in the lathe and I started turning, the wood shavings start to fly to the ceiling. They all opened up their mouth. They said, that German knows the work. He must come from a family from woodturners. And they all looked. They couldn't talk. The way I was turning out that big model, you know, from the blueprint.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the foreman, the boss stood in his office, was on a high-- in a higher room, like in these buildings. They have, like, a higher floor a little, with their offices. He looked through, and he saw they way I'm turning it. He didn't come out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then after the time, I figured, I got to go to my uncle. My uncle think, God knows what happened. I went out to lunch. So I walked over to Mr. [? Blitz, ?] was the name, the owner. Mr. [? Blitz, ?] I says, I got to go back to my uncle, to the shop. He says, don't go back. Finish the day here. I says, no, I cannot finish the day here. My uncle is going to know God knows what happened to me. I got to go. I have my-- my clothes there. This is my working clothes. My good clothes is there. I got to go and tell him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, OK, but leave the tools here. Don't take the tools with you. I'll take it in my office. He didn't want me to leave, to have security.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to my uncle, and I told him. He was a little happy and a little angry. He was happy and a little angry. And I said, how much, Mr. [? Blitz, ?] you planning to pay me? He says, we'll start out with $75. I-- from my uncle, I got $30. Here he's giving me $75. And as time goes on, he says, they're gonna get a raise $5, like, every other month.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I worked in-- for Mr. [? Blitz ?] for maybe five, six months. Then I saw the big noise on Lafayette and all the [INAUDIBLE]. And I saw the jealous in the shop. The old men didn't like me. You know, older people don't like when they see a young boy can make three times faster the job than they do, which I didn't want to do it, but I did my duty, you know. So they were jealous. If I was taken a sharpening stone to sharpen tools, they took it away. Even they find out there that I'm Jewish too. And they made all kind of remarks, you know, about the newcomers who came to America, you know, that they've taken away all the jobs from the Americans. They made bad remarks. And I saw it's not a place for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, I saw a ad in the newspaper, said they're looking for a woodturner in Seaford, Long Island. So I came to Long Island. And he was a gentleman, very nice person. He tried me out. He advertised he's a gentleman, but in the American paper, he couldn't get a woodturner. He advertised and advertised. Because woodturners was a trade almost in Jewish hands. OK, we're gonna have to stop a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.
